    2. D.2 Experiment design protocols
  12. E Additional Experiment Results

 

Appendix A Additional Background

Bayesian pruning.

More generally, the introduction of sparsity-inducing and shrinkage priors including Laplace (Seeger et al., 2007), Horseshoe (Carvalho et al., 2009), Spike-and-slab (Ray and Szabó, 2022) and complexity-based (Castillo and van der Vaart, 2012; Castillo et al., 2015; Martin et al., 2017) has been studied extensively for Bayesian pruning, variable, and model selection. Perhaps as a well-known bridge between frequentist and Bayesian ideas, the LASSO estimator is both interpreted as an M-estimator for high-dimensional settings or a Laplace shrinkage prior for sparsification (Hastie et al., 2015). Recent work includes shrinkage priors on model weights in combination with Bayesian neural networks (Louizos et al., 2017; Molchanov et al., 2017; Neklyudov et al., 2017; Ghosh et al., 2019; Nalisnick et al., 2021, 2019), and has also seen explicit use of the marginal likelihood for model sparsification (Immer et al., 2021; Bouchiat et al., 2023; Dhahri et al., 2024), albeit necessitating approximations via the Laplace.

Further related works.

Additional related works on SBL include Titsias and Lázaro-Gredilla (2014) for a variational inference approach and Rudy and Sapsis (2021) for a discussion on threshold design to strictly nullify down-weighted features. On heteroscedastic modelling, Helgøy et al. (2024) consider the RVM with a Laplace approximation, while Altamirano et al. (2024); Algikar and Mili (2023); Andrade and Takeda (2023) present interesting takes on robust GPs using generalized inference or non-standard likelihoods. Park et al. (2022) present a GP model with additional bias term for outlier identification.

Another interesting conceptual bridge can be drawn to robustness in principal component analysis (PCA), such as Candès et al. (2011), when viewed through a shared regularization pattern. Both approaches encourage parsimonious signal representation (via a low-rank component resp. sparse model parameters 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}}) and structured explanation of corruptions (via a sparse component resp. per-sample noise 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}}), and also share overlap in IRLS-type solver procedures. Nonetheless, robust PCA (Candès et al., 2011) requires distinct low-rank and corrupt components for (unsupervised) identifiability, while in our (supervised) setting each sample may be an important contributor and corrupted, with the marginal likelihood balancing model fit and robustness.

Further placement in existing literature.

An overview pertaining to key properties of interest is given in Tab. 1. The proposed joint ARD framework can be positioned at the intersection of two lines of research: sparse Bayesian learning and robust regression. On the model side, the ARD principle provides a well-established route to weight or feature sparsity and variable selection, with ties to compressed sensing (e.g. via pursuit algorithms), sparse GPs, and more broadly Bayesian pruning (e.g. regarding prior choices). On the data side, heteroscedastic noise modelling includes Bayesian and non-Bayesian robust regression, robust GPs, and heavy-tailed likelihoods and noise models. We ensure robustness by leveraging principles from the latter line of research, while operating within the ARD framework initially designed for the former, sparsity-focused line of research.

Appendix B Mathematical Details

We relate ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) to the dual objective ℒ~​(𝜸,𝝀)\tilde{\mathcal{L}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}), provide detailed derivations for all heteroscedastic SBL update rules (including a summary table in Tab. 5) and discuss how marginal likelihood optimization connects to the Bayesian learning rule (Khan and Rue, 2023) in linear models (§ B.8).

B.1 Formulation of the dual objective

We enumerate the primal objective from Eq. 3 as follows

−log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀)⏟(III)=n2​log⁡(2​π)+12​log⁡|Σ𝐲|⏟(II)+12​𝐲⊤​Σ𝐲−1​𝐲⏟(I)\underbrace{-\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}_{\text{(III)}}=\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)+\underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|}_{\text{(II)}}+\underbrace{\frac{1}{2}{\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}^{-1}\,{\mathbf{y}}}_{\text{(I)}}

and re-express each part following manipulations.

(I) Using the Woodbury identity (A+C​B​C⊤)−1=A−1−A−1​C​(B−1+C⊤​A−1​C)−1​C⊤​A−1 on Σ𝐲−1\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}^{-1} we obtain

Σ𝐲−1=(Λ+𝐗​Γ−1​𝐗⊤)−1=Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​(Γ+𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐗)−1⏟=Σ𝜽⁣∈ℝd×d​𝐗⊤​Λ−1=Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1.\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}^{-1}=(\Lambda+{\mathbf{X}}\,\Gamma^{-1}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top})^{-1}=\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\underbrace{(\Gamma+{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}})^{-1}}_{=\;\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\;\in\;\mathbb{R}^{d\times d}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}=\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}.

Plugging into the expression, we then have

𝐲⊤​Σ𝐲−1​𝐲=𝐲⊤​(Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1)​𝐲=𝐲⊤​Λ−1​𝐲−𝐲⊤​Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐲⏟=𝐲~⁣∈ℝd=𝐲⊤​Λ−1​𝐲−𝐲~⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐲~,{\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}^{-1}\,{\mathbf{y}}={\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\,(\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1})\,{\mathbf{y}}={\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}}\,-\,{\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\underbrace{{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}}}_{=\;\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}\;\in\;\mathbb{R}^{d}}={\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}}\,-\,\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}^{\top}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}},

where 𝐲~=𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐲\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}={\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}} becomes the data projection into weight space.

(II) Applying the matrix determinant lemma as |A+U​W​V⊤|=|W−1+V⊤​A−1​U|⋅|W|⋅|A| we obtain

log⁡|Σ𝐲|=log⁡(|Γ+𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐗|⋅|Γ−1|⋅|Λ|)=log⁡|Γ+𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐗|⏟=Σ𝜽−1−log⁡|Γ|+log⁡|Λ|,\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|=\log\left(|\Gamma+{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}|\cdot|\Gamma^{-1}|\cdot|\Lambda|\right)=\log\underbrace{|\Gamma+{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}|}_{=\;\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}}\,-\,\log|\Gamma|\,+\,\log|\Lambda|,

where again Σ𝜽\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}} emerges. Combining (I) and (II) to re-express (III), the dual objective (Eq. 4) is given by

ℒ~​(𝜸,𝝀)=−log⁡p​(𝐲~∣𝜸,𝝀)=n2​log⁡(2​π)+12​(log⁡|Σ𝜽−1|−log⁡|Γ|+log⁡|Λ|)+12​(𝐲⊤​Λ−1​𝐲−𝐲~⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐲~)≡log⁡|Σ𝜽−1|−log⁡|Γ|+log⁡|Λ|+(𝐲⊤​Λ−1​𝐲−𝐲~⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐲~).\begin{split}\tilde{{\mathcal{L}}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})&=-\log p(\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})\\ &=\frac{n}{2}\log(2\pi)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\log|\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}|-\log|\Gamma|+\log|\Lambda|\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left({\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}}\,-\,\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}^{\top}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}\right)\\ &\equiv\log|\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}|-\log|\Gamma|+\log|\Lambda|+\left({\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}}\,-\,\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}^{\top}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\tilde{{\mathbf{y}}}\right).\end{split}

B.2 Update rules from first-order optimality conditions

Consider the primal objective (Eq. 3) as

ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)=log⁡|Σ𝐲|+𝐲⊤​Π𝐲​𝐲,\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|+{\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}},

where we define Π𝐲=Σ𝐲−1\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}=\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}^{-1} for notational convenience moving forward.

Update for γj\gamma_{j}.

A first-order optimality condition for γj\gamma_{j} is given by

∂ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)∂γj=!0.\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\gamma_{j}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0.

Differentiation of the individual components yields that

∂log⁡|Σ𝐲|∂γj=trace​(Π𝐲​∂Σ𝐲∂γj)=−1γj2​𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j,∂Π𝐲∂γj=−Π𝐲​∂Σ𝐲∂γj​Π𝐲=−1γj2​Π𝐲​𝐱j⊤​𝐱j​Π𝐲,\frac{\partial\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|}{\partial\gamma_{j}}=\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,\frac{\partial\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}}{\partial\gamma_{j}})=-\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}^{2}}{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j},\qquad\frac{\partial\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}}{\partial\gamma_{j}}=-\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,\frac{\partial\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}}{\partial\gamma_{j}}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}=-\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}^{2}}\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}},

where 𝐱j{\mathbf{x}}_{j} denotes the jj-th column of 𝐗{\mathbf{X}}. Combined, we obtain that

∂ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)∂γj=−1γj2​(𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j−𝐲⊤​(Π𝐲​𝐱j⊤​𝐱j​Π𝐲)​𝐲)=−1γj2​(𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j−(𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐲)2)=!0.\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\gamma_{j}}=-\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}^{2}}\left({\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}-{\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\left(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\right){\mathbf{y}}\right)=-\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}^{2}}\left({\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}-\left({\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}\right)^{2}\right)\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0.

We now derive two identities to obtain more convenient expressions for the condition. First, using the posterior mean and covariance definitions we see that

𝝁𝜽=Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐲⇔Σ𝜽−1​𝝁𝜽=𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐲⇔Γ​𝝁𝜽=𝐗⊤​Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽),{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}=\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}\,{\mathbf{y}}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}={\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{y}}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\Gamma\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}={\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}),

and using Π𝐲=Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}=\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1} via Woodbury we observe that Π𝐲​𝐲=Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽)\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}=\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}) (see update derivation for λi\lambda_{i} below), which can be plugged into the right-hand side. Taking the jj-th component, we obtain the first identity as 𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐲=γj⋅μ𝜽,j{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}=\gamma_{j}\cdot\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}.

Next, we re-use the identity for Π𝐲\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}} and see that the inner product on the jj-th column is given by

𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j=𝐱j⊤​Λ−1​𝐱j−𝐱j⊤​Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐱j=uj−𝐮⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐮,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}={\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}-{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{x}}_{j}=u_{j}-{\mathbf{u}}^{\top}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{u}},

where we define 𝐮=𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐱j{\mathbf{u}}={\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{x}}_{j}. Additionally defining 𝐬j=Σ𝜽​𝐞j{\mathbf{s}}_{j}=\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{e}}_{j} as the jj-th column of Σ𝜽\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}, left-multiplying by 𝐮⊤{\mathbf{u}}^{\top} and solving we find the expression 𝐮⊤​𝐬j=1−γj⋅sj​j=1−γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j{\mathbf{u}}^{\top}{\mathbf{s}}_{j}=1-\gamma_{j}\cdot s_{jj}=1-\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}. Furthermore, substituting Π𝐲\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}} via Woodbury and simplifying using the identity Σ𝜽​(Γ+𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐗)=𝐈d\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,(\Gamma+{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}})={\mathbf{I}}_{d} we find the expression Π𝐲​𝐗=Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​Γ\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}=\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\Gamma, and for the jj-th column Π𝐲​𝐱j=Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​Γ​𝐞j=γj⋅Λ−1​𝐗​𝐬j\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}=\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\Gamma\,{\mathbf{e}}_{j}=\gamma_{j}\cdot\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\,{\mathbf{s}}_{j}. Therefore we obtain the final identity

𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j=γj​(𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐱j)⊤​𝐬j=γj⋅𝐮⊤​𝐬j=γj​(1−γj⋅sj​j)=γj​(1−γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j).{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}=\gamma_{j}({\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{x}}_{j})^{\top}{\mathbf{s}}_{j}=\gamma_{j}\cdot{\mathbf{u}}^{\top}{\mathbf{s}}_{j}=\gamma_{j}(1-\gamma_{j}\cdot s_{jj})=\gamma_{j}(1-\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}).

Plugging in both identities into the optimality condition, we find the update as

γj​(1−γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j)=γj2⋅μ𝜽,j2⇔(1−γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j)=γj⋅μ𝜽,j2⇔γjt+1=1−γjt⋅[Σ𝜽]j​jμ𝜽,j2,\gamma_{j}(1-\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj})=\gamma_{j}^{2}\cdot\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}\;\Leftrightarrow\;(1-\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj})=\gamma_{j}\cdot\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{1-\gamma_{j}^{t}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}}{\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}},

where γjt\gamma_{j}^{t} denotes the previous iterate on the right-hand side (and equates MacKay’s update).

Update for λi\lambda_{i}.

We follow the same general steps as above. A first-order optimality condition for λi\lambda_{i} is given by

∂ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)∂λi=!0.\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\lambda_{i}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0.

Differentiation of the individual components yields that

∂log⁡|Σ𝐲|∂λi=trace​(Π𝐲​𝐄i​i)=[Π𝐲]i​i,∂Π𝐲∂λi=−Π𝐲​𝐄i​i​Π𝐲\frac{\partial\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|}{\partial\lambda_{i}}=\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{E}}_{ii})=[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii},\qquad\frac{\partial\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}}{\partial\lambda_{i}}=-\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{E}}_{ii}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}

since Λ=diag​(𝝀)\Lambda=\text{diag}({\bm{\lambda}}) and ∂Σ𝐲∂λi=𝐄i​i\frac{\partial\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}}{\partial\lambda_{i}}={\mathbf{E}}_{ii}, with 𝐄i​i∈[0,1]n×n{\mathbf{E}}_{ii}\in[0,1]^{n\times n} denoting the basis with a single 11 at (i,i)(i,i). Combined, we obtain

∂ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)∂λi=[Π𝐲]i​i−𝐲⊤​(Π𝐲​𝐄i​i​Π𝐲)​𝐲=[Π𝐲]i​i−([Π𝐲​𝐲]i)2=!0.\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\lambda_{i}}=[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}-{\mathbf{y}}^{\top}\,(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{E}}_{ii}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}})\,{\mathbf{y}}=[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}-([\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}]_{i})^{2}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0.

We now re-use the Woodbury identity for Π𝐲\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}} to obtain more convenient expressions for the condition. Since Π𝐲=Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}=\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}, we multiply with 𝐲{\mathbf{y}} and observe that

Π𝐲​𝐲=Λ−1​𝐲−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐲=Λ−1​𝐲−Λ−1​𝐗​𝝁𝜽=Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽),\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}=\Lambda^{-1}\,{\mathbf{y}}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}\,{\mathbf{y}}=\Lambda^{-1}\,{\mathbf{y}}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}=\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}),

using the definition of the posterior mean. Defining 𝐫=(𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽){\mathbf{r}}=({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}) we see that [Π𝐲​𝐲]i=[Λ−1​𝐫]i=λi−1⋅ri[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}]_{i}=[\Lambda^{-1}\,{\mathbf{r}}]_{i}=\lambda^{-1}_{i}\cdot r_{i}. Taking the diagonal of the Woodbury identity, we see that

[Π𝐲]i​i=[Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1]i​i=λi−1−λi−2⋅qi,qi=𝐱i⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱i[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}=[\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}]_{ii}=\lambda^{-1}_{i}-\lambda^{-2}_{i}\cdot q_{i},\quad q_{i}={\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}

where 𝐱i⊤{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top} indicates the ii-th row of 𝐗{\mathbf{X}}. Plugging the two expressions into the condition, we obtain

∂ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)∂λi=[Π𝐲]i​i−([Π𝐲​𝐲]i)2=λi−1−λi−2⋅qi−(λi−1⋅ri)2=!0.\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\lambda_{i}}=[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}-([\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{y}}]_{i})^{2}=\lambda^{-1}_{i}-\lambda^{-2}_{i}\cdot q_{i}-(\lambda^{-1}_{i}\cdot r_{i})^{2}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0.

Multiplication with λi2\lambda_{i}^{2} and re-arranging yields the final update form

λit+1=ri2+qi=(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝝁𝜽)2+𝐱i⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱i.\lambda_{i}^{t+1}=r_{i}^{2}+q_{i}=(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}})^{2}+{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}.

B.3 Update rules for Expectation Maximization

We target the complete-data joint log-likelihood of (𝐲,𝜽)({\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\theta}}), i.e.

log⁡p​(𝐲,𝜽∣𝜸,𝝀)∝log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)+log⁡p​(𝜽∣𝜸),\log p({\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})\propto\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})+\log p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}}),

where 𝜽{\bm{\theta}} forms the latent variable. The E-step sees computing the posterior qt​(𝜽)=p​(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸t,𝝀t)q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})=p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}}^{t},{\bm{\lambda}}^{t}), where we condition on fixed parameters 𝜸t,𝝀t{\bm{\gamma}}^{t},{\bm{\lambda}}^{t} from the previous step. Updates for 𝜸,𝝀{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}} are obtained in the M-step by maximizing the expected joint under the current posterior, given by

Q​(𝜸,𝝀)=𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝐲,𝜽∣𝜸,𝝀)]=𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)]+𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝜽∣𝜸)].Q({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})]=\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})]+\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})].

Update for γj\gamma_{j}.

Considering dependencies on 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} and since p​(𝜽∣𝜸)=∏j=1d𝒩​(0,γj−1)p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})=\prod_{j=1}^{d}{\mathcal{N}}(0,\gamma_{j}^{-1}) we obtain that

Q​(𝜸,⋅)=𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝜽∣𝜸)]≡∑j=1d(log⁡γj−γj⋅𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[θj2]),Q({\bm{\gamma}},\cdot)=\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})]\equiv\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\log\gamma_{j}-\gamma_{j}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\theta_{j}^{2}]\right),

such that first-order optimality implies

∂Q​(𝜸,⋅)∂γj=1γj−𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[θj2]=!0⇔γjt+1=1𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[θj2]=1μ𝜽,j2+[Σ𝜽]j​j,\frac{\partial\,Q({\bm{\gamma}},\cdot)}{\partial\gamma_{j}}=\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}}-\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\theta_{j}^{2}]\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0\;\Leftrightarrow\;\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{1}{\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\theta_{j}^{2}]}=\frac{1}{\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}+[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}},

since qt​(𝜽)=𝒩​(𝝁𝜽,Σ𝜽)q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})={\mathcal{N}}({\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}},\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}) and we match its second moment.

Update for λi\lambda_{i}.

Considering dependencies on 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} and since p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)=∏i=1n𝒩​(𝐱i⊤​𝜽,λi)p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})=\prod_{i=1}^{n}{\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i}) we obtain that

Q​(⋅,𝝀)=𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)]≡∑i=1n(log⁡λi+1λi⋅𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2]),Q(\cdot,{\bm{\lambda}})=\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})]\equiv\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\log\lambda_{i}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})^{2}]\right),

such that first-order optimality implies

∂Q​(⋅,𝝀)∂λi=1λi−1λi2⋅𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2]=!0⇔λit+1=𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2]=(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝝁𝜽)2+𝐱i⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱i,\frac{\partial\,Q(\cdot,{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\lambda_{i}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}^{2}}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})^{2}]\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0\;\Leftrightarrow\;\lambda_{i}^{t+1}=\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})^{2}]=(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}})^{2}+{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i},

since qt​(𝜽)=𝒩​(𝝁𝜽,Σ𝜽)q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})={\mathcal{N}}({\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}},\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}) and 𝔼​[z2]=𝔼​[z]2+Var​(z)\mathbb{E}[z^{2}]=\mathbb{E}[z]^{2}+\text{Var}(z) by law of total variance applied to z=(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)z=(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}}).

Update for λ\lambda in the homoscedastic case.

Similarly to the heteroscedastic case above we obtain

Q​(⋅,𝝀)=𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)]≡n​log⁡λ+1λ⋅𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[∑i=1n(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2]=n​log⁡λ+1λ⋅𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[‖𝐲−𝐗​𝜽‖22],Q(\cdot,{\bm{\lambda}})=\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})]\equiv n\log\lambda+\frac{1}{\lambda}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})^{2}\right]=n\log\lambda+\frac{1}{\lambda}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}}\|_{2}^{2}],

and by first-order optimality we have

∂Q​(⋅,𝝀)∂λ=nλ−1λ2⋅𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[‖𝐲−𝐗​𝜽‖22]=!0⇔λt+1=1n​𝔼qt​(𝜽)​[‖𝐲−𝐗​𝜽‖22]=1n​(‖𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽‖22+trace​(𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤)),\frac{\partial\,Q(\cdot,{\bm{\lambda}})}{\partial\lambda}=\frac{n}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}\cdot\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}}\|_{2}^{2}]\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0\;\Leftrightarrow\;\lambda^{t+1}=\frac{1}{n}\,\mathbb{E}_{q_{t}({\bm{\theta}})}[\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}}\|_{2}^{2}]=\frac{1}{n}\left(\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\|_{2}^{2}+\text{trace}({\mathbf{X}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top})\right),

or more explicitly λt+1=1n​∑i=1n[(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝝁𝜽)2+𝐱i⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱i]\lambda^{t+1}=\frac{1}{n}\,\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}})^{2}+{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\right] as a simplified, sample-averaged estimate.

B.4 Update rules for MacKay’s updates

MacKay’s updates can be directly obtained from first-order optimality conditions, and we detail those derivations in § B.2. Alternatively, we can start from the EM updates in § B.3 and leverage simple fixed-point rearrangements (i.e. assuming convergence to the EM optima) to arrive at the same rules, which we show next.

Update for γj\gamma_{j}.

Starting from the EM update and multiplying by γj\gamma_{j} we obtain

γjt+1=1μ𝜽,j2+[Σ𝜽]j​j⇔1γjt+1=μ𝜽,j2+[Σ𝜽]j​j⇔γjγjt+1=γj⋅μ𝜽,j2+γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j.\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{1}{\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}+[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}^{t+1}}=\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}+[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\frac{\gamma_{j}}{\gamma_{j}^{t+1}}=\gamma_{j}\cdot\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}+\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}.

At a fixed point we then have γjt+1=γj\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\gamma_{j} and thus

1=γj⋅μ𝜽,j2+γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j⇔(1−γj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j)=γj⋅μ𝜽,j2⇔γjt+1=1−γjt⋅[Σ𝜽]j​jμ𝜽,j2,1=\gamma_{j}\cdot\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}+\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}\;\Leftrightarrow\;(1-\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj})=\gamma_{j}\cdot\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{1-\gamma_{j}^{t}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}}{\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j}^{2}},

recovering the optimality-based update.

Update for λi\lambda_{i}.

For the heteroscedastic case there is no simplification via fixed-point conditions, and so the update takes the same direct form as for EM, that is

λit+1=(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝝁𝜽)2+𝐱i⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱i.\lambda_{i}^{t+1}=(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}})^{2}+{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}.

Update for λ\lambda in the homoscedastic case.

We revisit the EM update given by λt+1=1n​(‖𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽‖22+trace​(𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤)){\lambda^{t+1}=\frac{1}{n}\left(\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\|_{2}^{2}+\text{trace}({\mathbf{X}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top})\right)} and observe that simplifications can be done to the trace term. In the homoscedastic setting the posterior covariance simplifies to Σ𝜽=(Γ+1λ​𝐗⊤​𝐗)−1\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}=(\Gamma+\frac{1}{\lambda}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{X}})^{-1}, and thus we see that 𝐗⊤​𝐗=λ​(Σ𝜽−1−Γ){\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{X}}=\lambda(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}-\Gamma). It follows for the trace term that

trace​(𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤)=trace​(Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​𝐗)=λ⋅(trace​(Σ𝜽​Σ𝜽−1)−trace​(Σ𝜽​Γ))=λ⋅(d−∑j=1dγj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j),\text{trace}({\mathbf{X}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top})=\text{trace}(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{X}})=\lambda\cdot\left(\text{trace}(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1})-\text{trace}(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\Gamma)\right)=\lambda\cdot\left(d-\sum_{j=1}^{d}\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}\right),

where trace​(Σ𝜽​Σ𝜽−1)=trace​(𝐈d)=d\text{trace}(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1})=\text{trace}({\mathbf{I}}_{d})=d. Plugging into the update, invoking the fixed-point condition such that λt+1=λ\lambda^{t+1}=\lambda, and re-arranging we obtain

λ⋅(n−d+∑j=1dγj⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j)=‖𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽‖22⇔λt+1=‖𝐲−𝐗​𝝁𝜽‖22n−∑j=1d(1−γjt⋅[Σ𝜽]j​j),\lambda\cdot\left(n-d+\sum_{j=1}^{d}\gamma_{j}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}\right)=\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\|_{2}^{2}\;\Leftrightarrow\;\lambda^{t+1}=\frac{\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\|_{2}^{2}}{n-\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(1-\gamma_{j}^{t}\cdot[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}\right)},

which aligns with the classical MacKay update found in the literature, e.g. see Tipping (2001), App. A.2.

B.5 Update rules for ℓ2\ell_{2}-IRLS

Rather than directly operating on ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) (Eq. 3), Wipf and Nagarajan (2007, 2010) employ a majorization-minimization strategy on an upper-bounding surrogate objective via IRLS. Following notation in Wipf and Nagarajan (2010), ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) can be equivalently expressed (up to constants) as

ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)=(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)⊤​Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)+gSBL​(𝜽),\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})+g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}),

where gSBL​(𝜽)≡min𝜸≥0⁡{𝜽⊤​Γ​𝜽+log⁡|Σ𝐲|}g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}})\equiv\min_{{\bm{\gamma}}\geq 0}\{{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,\Gamma\,{\bm{\theta}}+\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|\} forms a non-separable penalty term. As gSBL​(𝜽)g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}) is (componentwise) non-decreasing and concave in 𝜽2{\bm{\theta}}^{2} (and similarly for |𝜽||{\bm{\theta}}|, see ℓ1\ell_{1}-IRLS) minimization can be accomplished by iterative ℓ2\ell_{2}-reweighted least squares. For tractable updates, a quadratic upper bound on gSBL​(𝜽)g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}) is derived as

gSBL​(𝜽)≤𝜽⊤​Γ​𝜽+log⁡|Σ𝐲|≤𝜽⊤​Γ​𝜽+log⁡|Σ𝜽−1|−log⁡|Γ|+log⁡|Λ|≤𝜽⊤​Γ​𝜽+(𝐳⊤​𝜸−h∗​(𝐳))−log⁡|Γ|+log⁡|Λ|,g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}})\leq{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,\Gamma\,{\bm{\theta}}+\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|\leq{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,\Gamma\,{\bm{\theta}}+\log|\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}|-\log|\Gamma|\,+\,\log|\Lambda|\leq{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,\Gamma\,{\bm{\theta}}+({\mathbf{z}}^{\top}{\bm{\gamma}}-h^{*}({\mathbf{z}}))-\log|\Gamma|\,+\,\log|\Lambda|,

leveraging the determinant lemma and (concave) Fenchel duality of log⁡|Σ𝜽−1|\log|\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}| to admit a separable upper bound, and yielding the IRLS-style surrogate

ℒIRLS​(𝜸,𝝀;𝐳)=(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)⊤​Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)−h∗​(𝐳)+∑j=1d(γj⋅(θj2+zj)−log⁡γj)+log⁡|Λ|≥ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀).{\mathcal{L}}^{\text{IRLS}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}};{\mathbf{z}})=({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})-h^{*}({\mathbf{z}})+\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\gamma_{j}\cdot(\theta_{j}^{2}+z_{j})-\log\gamma_{j}\right)+\log|\Lambda|\geq\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}).

We refer to Wipf and Nagarajan (2010, 2007) for more details on the employed concave conjugate h∗​(𝐳)h^{*}({\mathbf{z}}) and the auxiliary-bound viewpoint (for the homoscedastic setting).

Updates for 𝐳,𝜽{\mathbf{z}},{\bm{\theta}}.

Treating 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} as fixed, the optimal value for auxiliary variables 𝐳{\mathbf{z}} is given by the slope of log⁡|Σ𝜽−1|\log|\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}| at current iterate 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} (Fenchel optimality condition), i.e.

zjt+1=∂log⁡|Σ𝜽−1|∂γj=trace​(Σ𝜽​∂Σ𝜽−1∂γj)=trace​(Σ𝜽​𝐄j​j)=[Σ𝜽]j​j.z_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{\partial\log|\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}|}{\partial\gamma_{j}}=\text{trace}(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\frac{\partial\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}^{-1}}{\partial\gamma_{j}})=\text{trace}(\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{E}}_{jj})=[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}.

Minimizing the surrogate w.r.t. 𝜽{\bm{\theta}} at fixed (𝜸,𝝀,𝐳)({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}},{\mathbf{z}}) and dropping constants yields the weighted ridge update

𝜽t+1=argmin𝜽(𝐲−𝐗𝜽)⊤Λ−1(𝐲−𝐗𝜽)+∑j=1dwj⋅θj2{\bm{\theta}}^{t+1}=\arg\min_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})+\sum_{j=1}^{d}w_{j}\cdot\theta_{j}^{2}

with ridge weights given by wj=γjw_{j}=\gamma_{j}, which admits a closed-form solution.

Update for γj\gamma_{j}.

For fixed (𝜽,𝝀,𝐳)({\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}},{\mathbf{z}}) minimizing each separable term dependent on 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} yields the optimality condition

∂∂γj​(γj⋅(θj2+zj)−log⁡γj)=(θj2+zj)−1γj=!0⇔γjt+1=1θj2+zj=1θj2+[Σ𝜽]j​j,\frac{\partial}{\partial\gamma_{j}}(\gamma_{j}\cdot(\theta_{j}^{2}+z_{j})-\log\gamma_{j})=(\theta_{j}^{2}+z_{j})-\frac{1}{\gamma_{j}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0\;\Leftrightarrow\;\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{1}{\theta_{j}^{2}+z_{j}}=\frac{1}{\theta_{j}^{2}+[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}},

characterizing the same optimality condition as the EM update rule in § B.3 (with θj=μ𝜽,j\theta_{j}=\mu_{{\bm{\theta}},j} at convergence). To recover the iterative update found in Wipf and Nagarajan (2010), we employ the Woodbury identity on Σ𝜽\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}} to obtain

Σ𝜽=(Γ+𝐗⊤​Λ−1​𝐗)−1=Γ−1−Γ−1​𝐗⊤​(Λ+𝐗​Γ−1​𝐗⊤)−1​𝐗​Γ−1=Γ−1−Γ−1​𝐗⊤​Π𝐲​𝐗​Γ−1,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}=(\Gamma+{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}})^{-1}=\Gamma^{-1}-\Gamma^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}(\Lambda+{\mathbf{X}}\Gamma^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top})^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Gamma^{-1}=\Gamma^{-1}-\Gamma^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}{\mathbf{X}}\Gamma^{-1},

and taking the diagonal element we see that

[Σ𝜽]j​j=[Γ−1−Γ−1​𝐗⊤​Π𝐲​𝐗​Γ−1]j​j=γj−1−γj−2⋅qj,qj=𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj}=[\Gamma^{-1}-\Gamma^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}{\mathbf{X}}\Gamma^{-1}]_{jj}=\gamma^{-1}_{j}-\gamma^{-2}_{j}\cdot q_{j},\quad q_{j}={\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}

where 𝐱j{\mathbf{x}}_{j} denotes the jj-th column of 𝐗{\mathbf{X}}. Plugging in the expression we obtain the update

γjt+1=(θj2+[Σ𝜽]j​j)−1=(θj2+(γjt)−1−(γjt)−2⋅qj)−1,\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=(\theta_{j}^{2}+[\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}]_{jj})^{-1}=(\theta_{j}^{2}+(\gamma^{t}_{j})^{-1}-(\gamma^{t}_{j})^{-2}\cdot q_{j})^{-1},

which matches the update rule found in Wipf and Nagarajan (2010), Eq. 29. Note that above steps do not rely on a particular parametrization of 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}}, permitting the same IRLS-style updates to hold in the heteroscedastic case.

Update for λi\lambda_{i}.

Based on characterization of the same optimality conditions as the EM update rules, we start from a true stationary point of ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) and apply the Woodbury identity to Σ𝐲\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}} (as also done in § B.2) to obtain the expression

[Π𝐲]i​i=[Λ−1−Λ−1​𝐗​Σ𝜽​𝐗⊤​Λ−1]i​i=λi−1−λi−2⋅qi,qi=𝐱i⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱i[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}=[\Lambda^{-1}-\Lambda^{-1}{\mathbf{X}}\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\Lambda^{-1}]_{ii}=\lambda^{-1}_{i}-\lambda^{-2}_{i}\cdot q_{i},\quad q_{i}={\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}

where 𝐱i⊤{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top} indicates the ii-th row of 𝐗{\mathbf{X}}. Thus qi=λi−λi2​[Π𝐲]i​iq_{i}=\lambda_{i}-\lambda_{i}^{2}\,[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii} plugged into the EM update yields

λit+1=(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2+qi=(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2+λit−(λit)2​[Π𝐲]i​i\lambda_{i}^{t+1}=(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{2}+q_{i}=(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{2}+\lambda_{i}^{t}-(\lambda_{i}^{t})^{2}\,[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}

as an iterative heteroscedastic update.

Update for λ\lambda in the homoscedastic case.

As for EM, the homoscedastic case collapses to a simple sample-averaged estimate, which using the above expression for qiq_{i} is given by

λt+1=1n​(‖𝐲−𝐗​𝜽‖22+λt−(λt)2⋅trace​(Π𝐲))=1n​∑i=1n(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2+λt−(λt)2n​∑i=1n[Π𝐲]i​i.\lambda^{t+1}=\frac{1}{n}\left(\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}}\|_{2}^{2}+\lambda^{t}-(\lambda^{t})^{2}\cdot\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}})\right)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{2}+\lambda^{t}-\frac{(\lambda^{t})^{2}}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}.

B.6 Update rules for ℓ1\ell_{1}-IRLS

As for the ℓ2\ell_{2} case, a majorization-minimization strategy on an upper-bounding surrogate objective is employed. First, using Wipf and Nagarajan (2010) we observe the same IRLS formulation of ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) as

ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)=(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)⊤​Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)+gSBL​(𝜽),\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})+g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}),

where gSBL​(𝜽)≡min𝜸≥0⁡{𝜽⊤​Γ​𝜽+log⁡|Σ𝐲|}g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}})\equiv\min_{{\bm{\gamma}}\geq 0}\{{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,\Gamma\,{\bm{\theta}}+\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|\} forms a non-separable penalty term. gSBL​(𝜽)g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}) is componentwise non-decreasing and concave in |𝜽||{\bm{\theta}}| (as we show more explicitly below), and thus permits minimization by iterative ℓ1\ell_{1}-reweighted least squares.

Obtaining a separable upper bound.

In contrast to the setting in Wipf and Nagarajan (2010) where 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} is ignored, we now desire a tractable and separable upper bound on gSBL​(𝜽)g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}) that induces ℓ1\ell_{1}-regularization terms in both 𝜸,𝝀{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}, achievable by similarly majorizing the concave term log⁡|Σ𝐲|\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}| with an affine Fenchel bound. For the concave function f​(𝐗)=log⁡|𝐗|f({\mathbf{X}})=\log|{\mathbf{X}}| on a symmetric positive-definite matrix 𝐗{\mathbf{X}} of size nn, we leverage the Fenchel identity

log⁡|𝐗|=min𝐏≻0⁡{trace​(𝐏𝐗)−log⁡|𝐏|−n},\log|{\mathbf{X}}|=\min_{{\mathbf{P}}\succ 0}\left\{\text{trace}({\mathbf{P}}{\mathbf{X}})-\log|{\mathbf{P}}|-n\right\},

whose minimizer is 𝐏∗=𝐗−1{\mathbf{P}}^{*}={\mathbf{X}}^{-1} (Boyd and Vandenberghe, 2004). This follows directly from the definition of the concave conjugate of f​(𝐗)f({\mathbf{X}}) as f∗​(𝐏)=min𝐗≻0⁡{⟨𝐏,𝐗⟩−f​(𝐗)}f^{*}({\mathbf{P}})=\min_{{\mathbf{X}}\succ 0}\left\{\langle{\mathbf{P}},{\mathbf{X}}\rangle-f({\mathbf{X}})\right\} with minimizer 𝐗∗=𝐏−1{\mathbf{X}}^{*}={\mathbf{P}}^{-1}, yielding f∗​(𝐏)=n+log⁡|𝐏|f^{*}({\mathbf{P}})=n\,+\,\log|{\mathbf{P}}|; and the equivalent relation f​(𝐗)=min𝐏≻0⁡{⟨𝐏,𝐗⟩−f∗​(𝐏)}f({\mathbf{X}})=\min_{{\mathbf{P}}\succ 0}\left\{\langle{\mathbf{P}},{\mathbf{X}}\rangle-f^{*}({\mathbf{P}})\right\} made explicit. Thus for any fixed 𝐏≻0{\mathbf{P}}\succ 0, we obtain the upper bound log⁡|𝐗|≤trace​(𝐏𝐗)−log⁡|𝐏|−n\log|{\mathbf{X}}|\leq\text{trace}({\mathbf{P}}{\mathbf{X}})-\log|{\mathbf{P}}|-n, with equality at 𝐏∗{\mathbf{P}}^{*}. Applied to 𝐗=Σ𝐲=Λ+𝐗​Γ−1​𝐗⊤{\mathbf{X}}=\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}=\Lambda+{\mathbf{X}}\,\Gamma^{-1}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top} and 𝐏=Σ𝐲−1=Π𝐲{\mathbf{P}}=\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}^{-1}=\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}, we obtain a tight linear upper bound up to constants555𝐏{\mathbf{P}} is held fixed within each iteration, hence −log⁡|𝐏|-\log|{\mathbf{P}}| acts as a constant and can be omitted during minimization. given by

log⁡|Σ𝐲|≤trace​(Π𝐲​Λ)+trace​(Π𝐲​𝐗​Γ−1​𝐗⊤)=∑i=1n([Π𝐲]i​i⋅λi)+∑j=1d([𝐗⊤​Π𝐲​𝐗]j​j⋅γj−1)=∑i=1nzi⋅λi+∑j=1dqj⋅γj−1,\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|\leq\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,\Lambda)+\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}\,\Gamma^{-1}\,{\mathbf{X}}^{\top})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left([\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}\cdot\lambda_{i}\right)+\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left([{\mathbf{X}}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{X}}]_{jj}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}z_{i}\cdot\lambda_{i}+\sum_{j=1}^{d}q_{j}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j},

with zi=[Π𝐲]i​iz_{i}=[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii} and qj=𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱jq_{j}={\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}. Note that parameters 𝜸,𝝀{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}} are now separable, and the coefficients zi,qjz_{i},q_{j} match the expressions obtained by separately tracing the Fenchel optimality condition for each parameter, i.e.

zit+1=∂log⁡|Σ𝐲|∂λi=trace​(Π𝐲​𝐄i​i)=[Π𝐲]i​i,qjt+1=∂log⁡|Σ𝐲|∂γj−1=trace​(Π𝐲​∂Σ𝐲∂γj−1)=𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱j.z_{i}^{t+1}=\frac{\partial\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|}{\partial\lambda_{i}}=\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{E}}_{ii})=[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii},\qquad q_{j}^{t+1}=\frac{\partial\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|}{\partial\gamma_{j}^{-1}}=\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,\frac{\partial\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}}{\partial\gamma_{j}^{-1}})={\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}.

Plugging the expression into gSBL​(𝜽)g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}), a final separable upper bound is obtained as

gSBL​(𝜽)≤𝜽⊤​Γ​𝜽+log⁡|Σ𝐲|≤∑i=1nzi⋅λi+∑j=1d(θj2⋅γj+qj⋅γj−1),g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}})\leq{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,\Gamma\,{\bm{\theta}}+\log|\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}}|\leq\sum_{i=1}^{n}z_{i}\cdot\lambda_{i}+\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\theta_{j}^{2}\cdot\gamma_{j}+q_{j}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j}\right),

yielding the surrogate

ℒIRLS​(𝜸,𝝀;𝐳,𝐪)=(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)⊤​Λ−1​(𝐲−𝐗​𝜽)+∑i=1nzi⋅λi+∑j=1d(θj2⋅γj+qj⋅γj−1)≥ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀).{\mathcal{L}}^{\text{IRLS}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}};{\mathbf{z}},{\mathbf{q}})=({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})+\sum_{i=1}^{n}z_{i}\cdot\lambda_{i}+\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\theta_{j}^{2}\cdot\gamma_{j}+q_{j}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j}\right)\geq\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}).

Update for γj\gamma_{j}.

Fixing all other parameters, minimization of each dependent term on 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} yields the optimality condition

∂∂γj​(θj2⋅γj+qj⋅γj−1)=θj2−qjγj2=!0⇔γjt+1=qjθj2=qj|θj|.\frac{\partial}{\partial\gamma_{j}}(\theta_{j}^{2}\cdot\gamma_{j}+q_{j}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j})=\theta_{j}^{2}-\frac{q_{j}}{\gamma_{j}^{2}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0\;\Leftrightarrow\;\gamma_{j}^{t+1}=\sqrt{\frac{q_{j}}{\theta_{j}^{2}}}=\frac{\sqrt{q_{j}}}{|\theta_{j}|}.

The corresponding weight-side contribution of gSBL​(𝜽)g_{\text{SBL}}({\bm{\theta}}) is upper-bounded by the ℓ1\ell_{1} penalty

min𝜸≥0{∑j=1d(θj2⋅γj+qj⋅γj−1)}=∑j=1d(θj2⋅qj|θj|+qj⋅|θj|qj))=2⋅∑j=1dqj⋅|θj|,\min_{{\bm{\gamma}}\geq 0}\left\{\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\theta_{j}^{2}\cdot\gamma_{j}+q_{j}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j}\right)\right\}=\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\theta_{j}^{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{q_{j}}}{|\theta_{j}|}+q_{j}\cdot\frac{|\theta_{j}|}{\sqrt{q_{j}}})\right)=2\cdot\sum_{j=1}^{d}\sqrt{q_{j}}\cdot|\theta_{j}|,

which is indeed non-decreasing and concave in each |θj||\theta_{j}|. Leveraging a different upper bound dependent on 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} only (and ignoring 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}}), the same update rule for γjt+1\gamma_{j}^{t+1} can be found in Wipf and Nagarajan (2010), Eq. 32.

Update for λi\lambda_{i}.

We first observe the appearence of 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} in the quadratic data term. Denoting residuals ri=yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽r_{i}=y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}}, we equivalently express the surrogate objective as

ℒIRLS​(𝜸,𝝀;𝐳,𝐪)=∑i=1n(ri2⋅λi−1+zi⋅λi)+∑j=1d(θj2⋅γj+qj⋅γj−1).{\mathcal{L}}^{\text{IRLS}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}};{\mathbf{z}},{\mathbf{q}})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_{i}^{2}\cdot\lambda_{i}^{-1}+z_{i}\cdot\lambda_{i}\right)+\sum_{j=1}^{d}\left(\theta_{j}^{2}\cdot\gamma_{j}+q_{j}\cdot\gamma^{-1}_{j}\right).

To obtain a data-side ℓ1\ell_{1} penalty, we similarly fix other parameters and pool all terms dependent on 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} to yield the optimality condition

∂∂λi​(ri2⋅λi−1+zi⋅λi)=zi−ri2λi2=!0⇔λit+1=ri2zi=|ri|zi,\frac{\partial}{\partial\lambda_{i}}(r_{i}^{2}\cdot\lambda_{i}^{-1}+z_{i}\cdot\lambda_{i})=z_{i}-\frac{r_{i}^{2}}{\lambda_{i}^{2}}\stackrel{{\scriptstyle\scalebox{1.0}{!}}}{{=}}0\;\Leftrightarrow\;\lambda_{i}^{t+1}=\sqrt{\frac{r_{i}^{2}}{z_{i}}}=\frac{|r_{i}|}{\sqrt{z_{i}}},

which results in the upper-bounding ℓ1\ell_{1} penalty

min𝝀≥0⁡{∑i=1n(ri2⋅λi−1+zi⋅λi)}=∑i=1n(ri2⋅zi|ri|+zi⋅|ri|zi)=2⋅∑i=1nzi⋅|ri|,\min_{{\bm{\lambda}}\geq 0}\left\{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_{i}^{2}\cdot\lambda_{i}^{-1}+z_{i}\cdot\lambda_{i}\right)\right\}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(r_{i}^{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{z_{i}}}{|r_{i}|}+z_{i}\cdot\frac{|r_{i}|}{\sqrt{z_{i}}}\right)=2\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sqrt{z_{i}}\cdot|r_{i}|,

also non-decreasing and concave in |ri||r_{i}| and |θj||\theta_{j}|. Note that in practice we alternate between updating 𝜽{\bm{\theta}} and 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}}, which are respectively fixed at current iterates. Thus the quadratic data term is kept and the derived ℓ1\ell_{1} penalty is added, rather than eliminating 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} in the same substep (which would result in a ‘pure’ ℓ1\ell_{1}-only surrogate).

Update for 𝜽{\bm{\theta}}.

Under fixed parameters, plugging in the above penalty expressions and minimizing the surrogate w.r.t. 𝜽{\bm{\theta}} then yields the double ℓ1\ell_{1}-regularized update

𝜽t+1=argmin𝜽(𝐲−𝐗𝜽)⊤Λ−1(𝐲−𝐗𝜽)+2∑j=1dwj⋅|θj|+ 2∑i=1nvi⋅|ri|,{\bm{\theta}}^{t+1}=\arg\min_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})^{\top}\,\Lambda^{-1}\,({\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}})+2\sum_{j=1}^{d}w_{j}\cdot|\theta_{j}|\,+\,2\sum_{i=1}^{n}v_{i}\cdot|r_{i}|,

with weights given by wj=qj=𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱jw_{j}=\sqrt{q_{j}}=\sqrt{{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}} and vi=zi=[Π𝐲]i​iv_{i}=\sqrt{z_{i}}=\sqrt{[\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}]_{ii}}. Since the residual penalty is non-separable in θj\theta_{j} there is no simple closed-form solution (as for the ℓ2\ell_{2} case), and the problem can be iteratively solved via split-variable or proximal gradient methods.

Update for λ\lambda in the homoscedastic case.

In the case where Λ=λ​𝐈n\Lambda=\lambda\,{\mathbf{I}}_{n} the Fenchel bound yields a λ\lambda-dependent part of the surrogate as 1λ​∑i=1nri2+λ​∑i=1nzi\frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n}r_{i}^{2}+\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{n}z_{i}, and minimization yields the global update

λt+1=∑i=1nri2∑i=1nzi=‖𝐲−𝐗​𝜽‖2trace​(Π𝐲).\lambda^{t+1}=\sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}r_{i}^{2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}z_{i}}}=\frac{\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}}\|_{2}}{\sqrt{\text{trace}(\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}})}}.

Using an alternating update scheme, minimization of the surrogate w.r.t. 𝜽{\bm{\theta}} collapses to a weighted LASSO problem of the form

𝜽t+1=arg⁡min𝜽⁡1λ​‖𝐲−𝐗​𝜽‖22+2​∑j=1dwj⋅|θj|,{\bm{\theta}}^{t+1}=\arg\min_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,\frac{1}{\lambda}\|{\mathbf{y}}-{\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}}\|_{2}^{2}+2\sum_{j=1}^{d}w_{j}\cdot|\theta_{j}|,

with weights wj=qj=𝐱j⊤​Π𝐲​𝐱jw_{j}=\sqrt{q_{j}}=\sqrt{{\mathbf{x}}_{j}^{\top}\,\Pi_{{\mathbf{y}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{j}}.

B.7 Summary of update rules

Following derivations for each procedure, a summary of key parameter update rules is presented below in Tab. 5. We highlight that despite taking different perspectives on the target objective, all three methods of EM, MacKay, and ℓ2\ell_{2}-IRLS recover the same update rules for 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} and 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} in the heteroscedastic case, bar algebraic arrangements (fixed-point for MacKay’s γj\gamma_{j}, Woodbury identities for ℓ2\ell_{2}-IRLS). This stresses the intuitive design of the emerging updates from taking a marginal likelihood perspective. Homoscedastic updates generally follow from collapsing individual components and employing algebraic simplifications, but take a similar, sample-averaged structural form to the heteroscedastic update.

B.8 Variational Learning for Linear Regression

To expand the scope beyond strictly conjugate (Gaussian) and linear models, we consider embedding ARD parameter optimization as part of a larger step-wise optimization procedure utilizing Variational Learning (Khan and Rue, 2023; Khan, 2025). To that end, we take a first step by demonstrating that, for our standard Gaussian linear regression model, the variational objective precisely recovers the marginal likelihood given in Eq. 3. This provides a stepping stone and a natural avenue to extensions of the current procedure in future work.

Notation.

Following above notation we describe the model and noise priors as p​(𝜽∣𝜸)=𝒩​(𝟎,Γ−1){p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})={\mathcal{N}}(\mathbf{0},\Gamma^{-1})} and p​(ϵ∣𝝀)=𝒩​(𝟎,Λ){p({\bm{\epsilon}}\mid{\bm{\lambda}})={\mathcal{N}}(\mathbf{0},\Lambda)}, the data likelihood as p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)=𝒩​(𝐗​𝜽,Λ)=∏i=1n𝒩​(𝐱i⊤​𝜽,λi)p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})={\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{X}}\,{\bm{\theta}},\,\Lambda)=\prod_{i=1}^{n}{\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i}), and the marginal likelihood as p​(𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀)=𝒩​(𝟎,Σ𝐲){p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})={\mathcal{N}}(\mathbf{0},\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}})}. The exact posterior is given as p​(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸,𝝀)=𝒩​(𝝁𝜽,Σ𝜽)p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})={\mathcal{N}}({\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}},\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}), whereas the approximate variational posterior is denoted q​(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸,𝝀)=𝒩​(𝐦,𝐒)q({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})={\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{m}},{\mathbf{S}}), or q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}}) in short.

Background.

q​(𝜽)∈𝒬q({\bm{\theta}})\in\mathcal{Q} approximates p​(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸,𝝀)p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) and is rendered tractable by originating from the exponential family 𝒬\mathcal{Q}, whose general p.d.f. takes the form p​(𝜽)=h​(𝜽)​exp⁡(⟨𝜼,𝐓​(𝜽)⟩−a​(𝜼))p({\bm{\theta}})=h({\bm{\theta}})\exp(\langle{\bm{\eta}},{\mathbf{T}}({\bm{\theta}})\rangle-a({\bm{\eta}})), with 𝐓​(𝜽)=[𝜽,𝜽​𝜽⊤]{\mathbf{T}}({\bm{\theta}})=[{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\theta}}{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}] the sufficient statistics. For our (full) Gaussian choice, q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}}) can be parametrized in different ways, namely by standard mean and covariance 𝝃=(𝐦,𝐒){\bm{\xi}}=({\mathbf{m}},{\mathbf{S}}), by natural parameters 𝜼=(𝐒−1​𝐦,−12​𝐒−1){\bm{\eta}}=({\mathbf{S}}^{-1}{\mathbf{m}},-\frac{1}{2}{\mathbf{S}}^{-1}), and by expectation parameters 𝝁=𝔼q​(𝜽)​[𝐓​(𝜽)]=(𝐦,𝐒+𝐦𝐦⊤){\bm{\mu}}=\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[{\mathbf{T}}({\bm{\theta}})]=({\mathbf{m}},{\mathbf{S}}+{\mathbf{m}}{\mathbf{m}}^{\top}). These parameterizations are equivalent and map between each other, which can be leveraged to rewrite our target objective and therein quantities.

The ELBO as target objective.

As common in variational inference, we consider the evidence lower bound (ELBO) as a surrogate maximization objective to the log-marginal likelihood. Since Eq. 3 targets minimization of its negative, we obtain the relation

ℒ(𝜸,𝝀)=−logp(𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀)≤−𝔼q​(𝜽)[logp(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)]+DKL[q(𝜽)∥p(𝜽∣𝜸)]=ℒELBO(𝜸,𝝀).\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=-\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})\leq-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})]+D_{\mathrm{KL}}[q({\bm{\theta}})\;\|\;p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})]=\mathcal{L}^{\text{ELBO}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}).

Recovery of the Bayesian learning rule in generality (see Khan and Rue (2023), Eq. 2) is obtained by rewriting

−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)]=−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log​∏i=1np​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]=∑i=1n𝔼q​(𝜽)​[−log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]=∑i=1n𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)],-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})]=-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\prod_{i=1}^{n}p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})\right]=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[-\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})]=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})],

with ℓi​(𝜽)=−log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)=−log⁡𝒩​(𝐱i⊤​𝜽,λi)\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})=-\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})=-\log{\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i}) the likelihood contribution as a per-sample loss term. Alternatively, using the posterior relation p​(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸,𝝀)=p​(𝐲∣𝜽,𝝀)⋅p​(𝜽∣𝜸)/𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀)p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\theta}},{\bm{\lambda}})\cdot p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})/{\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) the ELBO can be written as

ℒELBO(𝜸,𝝀)=DKL[q(𝜽)∥p(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸,𝝀)]−log𝒵(𝜸,𝝀),\mathcal{L}^{\text{ELBO}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=D_{\mathrm{KL}}[q({\bm{\theta}})\;\|\;p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})]-\log{\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}),

with 𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀)=p​(𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀){\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) the partition function independent of 𝜽{\bm{\theta}}. Clearly, for our conjugate Gaussian setting where 𝒬\mathcal{Q} contains the true posterior, the optimal value q∗​(𝜽)=p​(𝜽∣𝐲,𝜸,𝝀)q^{*}({\bm{\theta}})=p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\mathbf{y}},{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) minimizes the objective as DKL[⋅∥⋅]=0D_{\mathrm{KL}}[\cdot\|\cdot]=0, recovering the exact log-marginal likelihood.

Reformulation of the ELBO.

We now aim to make the same relation more explicit by parametrizing the above ELBO in terms of natural parameters 𝜼{\bm{\eta}}, as employed in variational learning. To that end, following Khan and Rue (2023) we may express q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}})—more specifically, the likelihood terms—using local site functions as

q​(𝜽)∝p​(𝜽∣𝜸)​∏i=1nexp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))​ with sites ​ti​(𝜽)=⟨∇~𝜼​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)],𝐓​(𝜽)⟩,q({\bm{\theta}})\propto p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})\;\prod_{i=1}^{n}\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))\;\text{ with sites }\;t_{i}({\bm{\theta}})=\langle\tilde{\nabla}_{{\bm{\eta}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})],{\mathbf{T}}({\bm{\theta}})\rangle,

where ℓi​(𝜽)\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}}) as above666Note that ∇~𝜼​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=∇~𝜼​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[−log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]=−∇~𝜼​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]\tilde{\nabla}_{{\bm{\eta}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]=\tilde{\nabla}_{{\bm{\eta}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[-\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})]=-\tilde{\nabla}_{{\bm{\eta}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})]. Each site ti​(𝜽)t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}) is expressed as an inner product between sufficient statistics 𝐓​(𝜽){\mathbf{T}}({\bm{\theta}}) and ∇~𝜼​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]\tilde{\nabla}_{{\bm{\eta}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})], the natural gradient with respect to natural parameters 𝜼{\bm{\eta}} evaluated at 𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]. This key quantity is rendered tractable later on, but for now consider general ti​(𝜽)t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}). We may then re-write the ELBO by expanding DKL[⋅∥⋅]D_{\mathrm{KL}}[\cdot\|\cdot] with the site parameterization of q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}}) to obtain

ℒELBO​(𝜸,𝝀)=−𝔼q​(𝜽)[log∏i=1np(yi∣𝜽,λi)]+DKL[q(𝜽)∥p(𝜽∣𝜸)]=−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log​∏i=1np​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]+𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡q​(𝜽)p​(𝜽∣𝜸)]=−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log​∏i=1np​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]+𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡p​(𝜽∣𝜸)​∏i=1nexp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))p​(𝜽∣𝜸)​𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀)]=𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡∏i=1nexp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))∏i=1np​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀)]=c​(𝜽)−log⁡𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀),\begin{split}\mathcal{L}^{\text{ELBO}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})&=-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\prod_{i=1}^{n}p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})\right]+D_{\mathrm{KL}}[q({\bm{\theta}})\;\|\;p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})]\\ &=-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\prod_{i=1}^{n}p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})\right]+\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\frac{q({\bm{\theta}})}{p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})}\right]\\ &=-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\prod_{i=1}^{n}p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})\right]+\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\frac{p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})\,\prod_{i=1}^{n}\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))}{p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})\,{\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})}\right]\\ &=\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n}\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))}{\prod_{i=1}^{n}p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})}\right]-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\log{\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})]\\ &=c({\bm{\theta}})-\log{\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}),\end{split}

where c​(𝜽)=∑i=1n𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡exp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]c({\bm{\theta}})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\frac{\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))}{p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})}\right] and 𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀){\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) forms the partition function of q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}}). The first term can also be rearranged to c​(𝜽)=∑i=1n𝔼q​(𝜽)​[−log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)+log⁡exp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))]=∑i=1n𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)−ti​(𝜽)]c({\bm{\theta}})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[-\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})+\log\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))\right]=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}})\right], and is interpretable as a posterior correction term (Khan, 2025). Thus reparametrization in terms of natural parameters is made apparent through ti​(𝜽)t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}), and the expression is rendered tractable if we can compute the natural gradients, as shown next.

Approximation of natural gradients.

The natural gradient terms can be rendered computable in several steps, as detailed in Khan and Rue (2023). First, reparametrization from 𝜼{\bm{\eta}} to expectation parameters 𝝁{\bm{\mu}} simplifies natural gradients to render standard gradients. Next, expression in terms of (𝐦,𝐒)({\mathbf{m}},{\mathbf{S}}) and use of Bonnet’s and Price’s theorems returns terms using first and second-order derivatives of 𝜽{\bm{\theta}}. Finally, the delta method can be used to approximate the expectations by point estimates evaluated at the mean. That is, computability of the two natural gradients is given by the outlined steps as

∇~η1​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=Reparam.​∇μ1𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=Reparam.​∇𝐦𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]−2​[∇𝐒𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]]​𝐦=Bonnet’s Thm.​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[∇𝜽ℓi​(𝜽)]−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[∇𝜽2ℓi​(𝜽)]​𝐦≈Delta method​∇𝜽ℓi​(𝜽)|𝜽=𝐦−[∇𝜽2ℓi​(𝜽)|𝜽=𝐦]​𝐦,∇~η2​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=Reparam.​∇μ2𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=Reparam.​∇𝐒𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=Price’s Thm.​12​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[∇𝜽2ℓi​(𝜽)]≈Delta method​12​∇𝜽2ℓi​(𝜽)|𝜽=𝐦.\begin{split}\tilde{\nabla}_{\eta_{1}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]&\overset{\text{Reparam.}}{=}\nabla_{\mu_{1}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]\\ &\overset{\text{Reparam.}}{=}\nabla_{{\mathbf{m}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]-2\,[\nabla_{{\mathbf{S}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]]\,{\mathbf{m}}\\ &\overset{\text{Bonnet's Thm.}}{=}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\nabla_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\nabla^{2}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]\,{\mathbf{m}}\\ &\overset{\text{Delta method}}{\approx}\nabla_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})|_{{\bm{\theta}}={\mathbf{m}}}-[\nabla^{2}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})|_{{\bm{\theta}}={\mathbf{m}}}]\,{\mathbf{m}},\\ \tilde{\nabla}_{\eta_{2}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]&\overset{\text{Reparam.}}{=}\nabla_{\mu_{2}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]\\ &\overset{\text{Reparam.}}{=}\nabla_{{\mathbf{S}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]\\ &\overset{\text{Price's Thm.}}{=}\frac{1}{2}\,\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\nabla^{2}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]\\ &\overset{\text{Delta method}}{\approx}\frac{1}{2}\,\nabla^{2}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})|_{{\bm{\theta}}={\mathbf{m}}}\,.\\ \end{split}

Thus, final approximations require merely gradient and Hessian evaluations at the variational posterior’s mean 𝐦{\mathbf{m}}, which are relatively straightforward to obtain.

Recovering the marginal likelihood objective.

Given pratically computable terms, we can now show that for our choice of Gaussian variational posterior and Gaussian linear regression model the reformulated ELBO objective also exactly recovers the marginal likelihood. We start by explicitly computing the natural gradients using above approximations. For our Gaussian linear model we then have

ℓi​(𝜽)=−log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)=−log⁡𝒩​(𝐱i⊤​𝜽,λi)=12​log⁡(2​π​λi)+12​λi​(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2,∇𝜽ℓi​(𝜽)=−1λi​(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)​𝐱i,∇𝜽2ℓi​(𝜽)=1λi​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤.\begin{split}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})=-&\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})=-\log{\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})=\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\lambda_{i})+\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})^{2},\\ &\nabla_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})=-\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i},\qquad\nabla^{2}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})=\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}.\end{split}

To compute the natural gradients, we follow the rules outlined above. As ℓi​(𝜽)\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}}) is quadratic in 𝜽{\bm{\theta}}, the expectations of ∇𝜽ℓi​(𝜽)\nabla_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}}) and ∇𝜽2ℓi​(𝜽)\nabla^{2}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}}) under Gaussian q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}}) are exact (affine/constant), thus we can skip the delta approximation. Plugging in the derivatives then yields the natural gradient terms

∇~η1​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=𝔼q​(𝜽)​[−1λi​(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)​𝐱i]−𝔼q​(𝜽)​[1λi​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤]​𝐦=−1λi​(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝐦)​𝐱i−(1λi​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤)​𝐦=−yiλi​𝐱i,∇~η2​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)]=12​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[1λi​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤]=12​λi​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤.\begin{split}\tilde{\nabla}_{\eta_{1}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]=\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[-\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\right]&-\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\right]\,{\mathbf{m}}=-\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\mathbf{m}})\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}-(\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top})\,{\mathbf{m}}=-\frac{y_{i}}{\lambda_{i}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i},\\ \tilde{\nabla}_{\eta_{2}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})]&=\frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{i}}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\right]=\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}.\\ \end{split}

We can now verify a given site function to observe the form

log⁡exp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))=−⟨∇~𝜼​𝔼q​(𝜽)​[ℓi​(𝜽)],𝐓​(𝜽)⟩=−(−yiλi​𝐱i⊤​𝜽+12​λi​𝜽⊤​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤​𝜽)=yiλi​𝐱i⊤​𝜽−12​λi​𝜽⊤​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤​𝜽,\log\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))=-\langle\tilde{\nabla}_{{\bm{\eta}}}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}[\ell_{i}({\bm{\theta}})],{\mathbf{T}}({\bm{\theta}})\rangle=-(-\frac{y_{i}}{\lambda_{i}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}}\,+\,\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}})=\frac{y_{i}}{\lambda_{i}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}}\,-\,\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}},

and expanding the quadratic likelihood term we also see that

log⁡p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)=−12​log⁡(2​π​λi)−12​λi​(yi−𝐱i⊤​𝜽)2=−12​log⁡(2​π​λi)−12​λi​(yi2−2​yi​𝐱i⊤​𝜽+𝜽⊤​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤​𝜽)=(−12​log⁡(2​π​λi)−yi22​λi)+yiλi​𝐱i⊤​𝜽−12​λi​𝜽⊤​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤​𝜽=Ci+yiλi​𝐱i⊤​𝜽−12​λi​𝜽⊤​𝐱i​𝐱i⊤​𝜽,\begin{split}\log p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})&=-\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\lambda_{i})-\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}(y_{i}-{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})^{2}\\ &=-\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\lambda_{i})-\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}(y_{i}^{2}-2y_{i}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}}+{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}})\\ &=\left(-\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\lambda_{i})-\frac{y_{i}^{2}}{2\lambda_{i}}\right)+\frac{y_{i}}{\lambda_{i}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}}\,-\,\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}}\\ &=C_{i}+\frac{y_{i}}{\lambda_{i}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}}\,-\,\frac{1}{2\lambda_{i}}{\bm{\theta}}^{\top}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}\,{\bm{\theta}},\\ \end{split}

with 𝜽{\bm{\theta}}-independent constant term CiC_{i}. Thus we immediately observe that exp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))∝p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))\propto p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i}), and therefore c​(𝜽)=∑i=1n𝔼q​(𝜽)​[log⁡exp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))p​(yi∣𝜽,λi)]=0c({\bm{\theta}})=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbb{E}_{q({\bm{\theta}})}\left[\log\frac{\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))}{p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})}\right]=0 up to constants C1,…,CnC_{1},\dots,C_{n}. Since the sites parametrized by natural gradients match the true likelihood factors, q​(𝜽)q({\bm{\theta}}) matches the optimal q∗​(𝜽)q^{*}({\bm{\theta}}) whose factorization yields

q∗​(𝜽)∝p​(𝜽∣𝜸)​∏i=1nexp⁡(−ti​(𝜽))∝p​(𝜽∣𝜸)​∏i=1np​(yi∣𝜽,λi)=p​(𝜽,𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀),q^{*}({\bm{\theta}})\propto p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})\;\prod_{i=1}^{n}\exp(-t_{i}({\bm{\theta}}))\propto p({\bm{\theta}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}})\;\prod_{i=1}^{n}p(y_{i}\mid{\bm{\theta}},\lambda_{i})=p({\bm{\theta}},{\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}),

and whose corresponding partition function is 𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀)=p​(𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀){\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}). It follows directly for the reformulated ELBO that ℒELBO​(𝜸,𝝀)=c​(𝜽)−log⁡𝒵​(𝜸,𝝀)=0−log⁡p​(𝐲∣𝜸,𝝀)=ℒ​(𝜸,𝝀)\mathcal{L}^{\text{ELBO}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=c({\bm{\theta}})-\log{\mathcal{Z}}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=0-\log p({\mathbf{y}}\mid{\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}})=\mathcal{L}({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}) recovers the exact marginal likelihood objective (Eq. 3), affirming that variational learning is amenable to ARD parameter optimization.

Appendix C Algorithmic Details

Appendix D Additional Experiment Details

The code to reproduce experiments is made available at https://github.com/alextimans/robust-sbl. Additional clarifying details are given below.

Effective support size (ESS).

Given generic nonnegative relevance scores {ri}i=1m\{r_{i}\}_{i=1}^{m}, i.e. ri=1/γir_{i}=1/\gamma_{i} for weight relevance and ri=1/λir_{i}=1/\lambda_{i} for data relevance, we first convert them into a probability mass function by normalization,

pi=ri∑j=1mrj,p_{i}=\frac{r_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m}r_{j}}\,,

optionally using a small ϵ>0\epsilon>0 and clipping for numerical stability. We then compute the Shannon entropy

H​(p)=−∑i=1mpi​log⁡(pi)H(p)\;=\;-\sum_{i=1}^{m}p_{i}\log(p_{i})

quantifying how dispersed the relevance mass is. Exponentiating yields perplexity as Neff=exp⁡(H​(p))N_{\mathrm{eff}}=\exp(H(p)), denoting an effective number of active elements, and we report its normalized quantity ESS=Neff/m∈(0,1]\mathrm{ESS}=N_{\mathrm{eff}}/m\in(0,1] as the effective support size. ESS approaches 11 for uniform relevance and becomes small when relevance concentrates on few elements, matching our sparsity-inducing interpretation for (𝜸,𝝀)({\bm{\gamma}},{\bm{\lambda}}). Finally, entropy-based ESS is related but distinct from the effective sample size commonly used in Monte Carlo methods and importance sampling: our definition corresponds to the Shannon (order-11) effective number, whereas a popular alternative is the order-22 quantity Neff(2)=1/∑ipi2N_{\mathrm{eff}}^{(2)}=1/\sum_{i}p_{i}^{2} (Martino et al., 2017).

Posterior predictive noise estimate.

The posterior predictive distribution for a new test input 𝐱∗∈ℝd×1{\mathbf{x}}_{*}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times 1} is given by

p​(𝐲∗∣𝐱∗,𝐗,𝐲)=𝒩​(𝐲∗∣μ∗,λ∗)withμ∗=𝐱∗⊤​𝝁𝜽andλ∗=λb+𝐱∗⊤​Σ𝜽​𝐱∗p({\mathbf{y}}_{*}\mid{\mathbf{x}}_{*},{\mathbf{X}},{\mathbf{y}})={\mathcal{N}}({\mathbf{y}}_{*}\mid\mu_{*},\;\lambda_{*})\quad\text{with}\quad\mu_{*}={\mathbf{x}}_{*}^{\top}{\bm{\mu}}_{{\bm{\theta}}}\quad\text{and}\quad\lambda_{*}=\lambda_{b}+{\mathbf{x}}_{*}^{\top}\,\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}}\,{\mathbf{x}}_{*}

denoting posterior predictive mean and variance, respectively. In particular, the latter requires selecting a base noise level λb\lambda_{b} that summarizes expected observation noise at test time, and which is a priori unknown. Assuming no distribution shift, we use a plug-in estimate based on the learned training sample variances 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} via data ARD. Setting λb=mean​(𝝀)\lambda_{b}=\mathrm{mean}({\bm{\lambda}}) is conservative in the sense that it aggregates across all samples, thus a small number of large λi\lambda_{i} values (flagged as unreliable by data ARD) can only increase λb\lambda_{b} and hence inflate predictive uncertainty rather than overstate confidence. A useful alternative is to employ a trimmed mean plug-in estimate to reduce sensitivity to extreme values when data contamination is subsumed, i.e. λb=mean​({λi:λi∈[qα,q1−α]})\lambda_{b}=\mathrm{mean}(\{\lambda_{i}:\lambda_{i}\in[q_{\alpha},q_{1-\alpha}]\}) where qαq_{\alpha} and q1−αq_{1-\alpha} may be pre-selected fixed empirical quantiles of {λi}i=1n\{\lambda_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n} (e.g. 5% and 95%). Importantly, both choices are computed solely from learned variances 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} and do not require any outlier ground truth labels or even prior knowledge of the contamination rate.

D.1 Practical implementation of update rules

Stable learning of closed-form SBL updates benefits from several practical safeguards. First, all matrix operations required for posterior moments (inversions, diagonals, and quadratic forms) are implemented via Cholesky factorization and triangular solves, yielding both improved numerical stability and computational efficiency over explicit inversion. Second, we damp iterative parameter updates using an exponential moving average as

𝜸t+1←(1−ηγ)​𝜸t+ηγ​𝜸newt+1,𝝀t+1←(1−ηλ)​𝝀t+ηλ​𝝀newt+1,ηγ∈(0,1],ηλ∈(0,1],{\bm{\gamma}}^{t+1}\leftarrow(1-\eta_{\gamma}){\bm{\gamma}}^{t}+\eta_{\gamma}{\bm{\gamma}}_{\mathrm{new}}^{t+1},\qquad{\bm{\lambda}}^{t+1}\leftarrow(1-\eta_{\lambda}){\bm{\lambda}}^{t}+\eta_{\lambda}{\bm{\lambda}}_{\mathrm{new}}^{t+1},\qquad\eta_{\gamma}\in(0,1],\eta_{\lambda}\in(0,1],

which improves conditioning and smooths optimization trajectories, analogous to the learning rate in gradient-based schemes. In practice we found strong damping in the range of η∈[5⋅10−4,2⋅10−2]\eta\in[5\cdot 10^{-4},2\cdot 10^{-2}] to be necessary. Third, we clip extreme values to preserve positivity and avoid ill-conditioning, e.g. ϵmin∈[10−6,10−3]\epsilon_{\min}\in[10^{-6},10^{-3}] and ϵmax∈[102,106]\epsilon_{\max}\in[10^{2},10^{6}], and add a small magnitude-adaptive jitter to Σ𝐲\Sigma_{{\mathbf{y}}} and Σ𝜽\Sigma_{{\bm{\theta}}} when required for robust factorization. Fourth, optimization convergence is monitored via a relative ℓ∞\ell_{\infty} change criterion on log-parameters for scale-invariance,

‖log⁡𝜸t+1−log⁡𝜸t‖∞1+‖log⁡𝜸t+1‖∞<ϵreland‖log⁡𝝀t+1−log⁡𝝀t‖∞1+‖log⁡𝝀t+1‖∞<ϵrel,\frac{\|\log{\bm{\gamma}}^{t+1}-\log{\bm{\gamma}}^{t}\|_{\infty}}{1+\|\log{\bm{\gamma}}^{t+1}\|_{\infty}}<\epsilon_{\mathrm{rel}}\quad\text{and}\quad\frac{\|\log{\bm{\lambda}}^{t+1}-\log{\bm{\lambda}}^{t}\|_{\infty}}{1+\|\log{\bm{\lambda}}^{t+1}\|_{\infty}}<\epsilon_{\mathrm{rel}},

combined with a small patience window (ϵrel=10−6\epsilon_{\mathrm{rel}}=10^{-6} for 5 patience steps). Empirically, we found this to be more reliable than loss-based stopping on the objective. For ℓ1\ell_{1}-IRLS we warm-start the inner convex subproblem (solved via ADMM) from the current iterate 𝜽t{\bm{\theta}}^{t} rather than re-optimizing from scratch. Finally, we also warm-start data ARD by initially updating only 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} for several iterations before enabling 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} (in the range of 50−30050-300 steps), and subsequently updating 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} only every K∈[2,5]K\in[2,5] outer iterations (rather than at every step), which yields a stronger initial fit and reduces the risk of overfit.

Introducing heteroscedastic noise expands the parameterization from a single scalar noise level to nn additional free variance parameters, substantially increasing flexibility and, with it, the risk of overfitting. In particular, the model risks prematurely ‘explaining away’ residual structure by inflating individual λi\lambda_{i} values instead of improving the predictive mean, a well-known tendency in heteroscedastic regression that can be exacerbated in highly expressive models (Cawley and Talbot, 2010; Grünwald et al., 2017; Wong-Toi et al., 2024). As a result, the learning dynamics of joint ARD can be sensitive to hyperparameters and numerical choices, and the above regularization mechanisms (damping, clipping, jittering, and robust stopping) are often necessary to obtain well-conditioned posteriors and meaningful convergence results in practice. Strongly misspecified priors can have a notable impact on convergence speed and recovery, an effect more pronounced for noise variances 𝝀{\bm{\lambda}} (which live on the residual scale) and less so for weight precisions 𝜸{\bm{\gamma}} (which live on their own scale). Nonetheless, we found weakly-informative choices to consistently work well across different algorithms and experiments, which in practice may look like a fixed scalar initial value (e.g. s=0.1s=0.1) across parameters.

D.2 Experiment design protocols

Synthetic experiment (§ 5.1).

We generate linear regression data with sparse ground-truth weights and uncorrelated Gaussian features. For each trial, we sample a support set S⊆{1,…,d}S\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\} uniformly at random and draw nonzero weights θj∼𝒩​(0,1)\theta_{j}\sim{\mathcal{N}}(0,1) for j∈Sj\in S, with θj=0\theta_{j}=0 otherwise. Features are sampled i.i.d. as 𝐗∈ℝn×d{\mathbf{X}}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d} with rows 𝐱i∼𝒩​(𝟎,𝐈){\mathbf{x}}_{i}\sim{\mathcal{N}}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I}). Observation noise is Gaussian with a base scale σ\sigma. We then sample an outlier index set 𝒪⊂{1,…,n}\mathcal{O}\subset\{1,\dots,n\} uniformly without replacement of size |𝒪|=⌊ρ⋅n⌋|\mathcal{O}|=\lfloor\rho\cdot n\rfloor, where ρ∈[0,1]\rho\in[0,1] is the contamination fraction. This set is assigned inflated variance by a multiplier mm. The targets are then generated as

yi=𝐱i⊤​𝜽+ϵi,ϵi∼{𝒩​(0,σ2),i∉𝒪,𝒩​(0,(m​σ)2),i∈𝒪.y_{i}={\mathbf{x}}_{i}^{\top}{\bm{\theta}}+\epsilon_{i},\qquad\epsilon_{i}\sim\begin{cases}{\mathcal{N}}(0,\sigma^{2}),&i\notin\mathcal{O},\\ {\mathcal{N}}(0,(m\sigma)^{2}),&i\in\mathcal{O}.\end{cases}

We return (𝐗,𝐲)({\mathbf{X}},{\mathbf{y}}) together with the ground-truth supports SS and 𝒪\mathcal{O} for evaluation. To generate the weight recovery heatmap in Fig. 3 we fix ρ=0.2,m=10\rho=0.2,m=10 and vary |S||S| and σ\sigma, whereas to generate the data recovery heatmap we fix |S|=0.2⋅d,σ=0.2|S|=0.2\cdot d,\sigma=0.2 and vary ρ\rho and mm. Throughout, n=500,d=50n=500,d=50 remain fixed and test samples ntest=1000n_{\text{test}}=1000 for predictive evaluation are generated entirely uncorrupted.

Tabular regression benchmarks (§ 5.2).

Training targets 𝐲{\mathbf{y}} are standardized to zero mean and unit variance. We then sample an outlier index set 𝒪⊂{1,…,n}\mathcal{O}\subset\{1,\dots,n\} uniformly without replacement of size |𝒪|=⌊ρ⋅n⌋|\mathcal{O}|=\lfloor\rho\cdot n\rfloor, where ρ∈[0,1]\rho\in[0,1] is the contamination fraction. For each i∈𝒪i\in\mathcal{O} we draw a sign si∈{−1,+1}s_{i}\in\{-1,+1\} i.i.d. Rademacher and add a signed perturbation of amplitude a>0a>0:

y~i=yi+a​si​δi,i∈𝒪,\tilde{y}_{i}\;=\;y_{i}+a\,s_{i}\,\delta_{i},\qquad i\in\mathcal{O},

where δi∼𝒩​(1,0.252)\delta_{i}\sim{\mathcal{N}}(1,0.25^{2}), yielding heterogeneous outlier magnitudes around aa while most y~i=yi\tilde{y}_{i}=y_{i} for i∉𝒪i\notin\mathcal{O} remain uncorrupted. The outlier amplitude is fixed at a=3.0a=3.0, and we report results for ρ=0\rho=0 (uncontaminated) and ρ=0.1\rho=0.1 (mild contamination).

The evaluated datasets in original sample size and feature dimension are: Boston (n=506,d=13n=506,d=13), Carbon (n=10721,d=5n=10721,d=5), Concrete (n=1030,d=8)n=1030,d=8), Elevators (n=16599,d=18n=16599,d=18), Energy (n=768,d=8n=768,d=8), Kin8nm (n=8192,d=8n=8192,d=8), Power (n=9568,d=4n=9568,d=4), Protein (n=45730,d=9n=45730,d=9), and Yacht (n=308,d=6n=308,d=6). We consistently randomly split 20%20\% for hold-out test evaluation (uncontaminated) and cap datasets with large training splits to a fixed maximum nmax=2000n_{\max}=2000 for computational efficiency (again, sampled at random). Otherwise we use the full training set. For datasets with multiple targets (Carbon with 3 targets, Energy with 2 targets) we only regress on the first target and omit the others.

Sparse kernel regression (§ 5.3).

The experiment follows the same general protocol as for tabular regression above, the key difference being a switch from random Fourier features to a kernel-based feature matrix 𝚽∈ℝn×n\mathbf{\Phi}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n} instantiating the RVM.

Neural network regression (§ 5.4).

Denoting the log-transformed count label as zi=log⁡(1+yi)z_{i}=\log(1+y_{i}), we form a high-count pool 𝒫\mathcal{P} as the indices of the top ⌊ρpool⋅n⌋\lfloor\rho_{\mathrm{pool}}\cdot n\rfloor values of {zi}i=1n\{z_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n}, and sample an outlier set 𝒪⊆𝒫\mathcal{O}\subseteq\mathcal{P} uniformly at random with |𝒪|=⌊ρout⋅|𝒫|⌋|\mathcal{O}|=\lfloor\rho_{\mathrm{out}}\cdot|\mathcal{P}|\rfloor. We then contaminate targets in log-space additively via

z~i=zi+ϵi,ϵi∼{𝒩​(0,σin2),i∉𝒪,𝒩​(0,σout2),i∈𝒪.\tilde{z}_{i}\;=\;z_{i}+\epsilon_{i},\qquad\epsilon_{i}\sim\begin{cases}{\mathcal{N}}(0,\sigma_{\mathrm{in}}^{2}),&i\notin\mathcal{O},\\ {\mathcal{N}}(0,\sigma_{\mathrm{out}}^{2}),&i\in\mathcal{O}.\end{cases}

The pool fraction is fixed at ρpool=0.4\rho_{\mathrm{pool}}=0.4 and the contamination fraction at ρout=0.5\rho_{\mathrm{out}}=0.5, yielding an effective contamination rate of ρpool⋅ρout=0.2\rho_{\mathrm{pool}}\cdot\rho_{\mathrm{out}}=0.2. The inlier scale is set to σin=0.0\sigma_{\mathrm{in}}=0.0 (no contamination) while the outlier scale is fixed at σout=0.45\sigma_{\mathrm{out}}=0.45.

Regarding the ShanghaiTech dataset, we only make use of the consistent ‘part B’ set (n=716n=716) to avoid test-time distribution shifts caused by web-crawled crowd scenes in ‘part A’. The data is randomly split into 80% train and 20% test samples (uncontaminated). For DINO-2 features we employ a fixed pre-trained backbone model DINOv2-ViT-S/14 (d=384d=384, see https://github.com/facebookresearch/dinov2) with no additional finetuning and access image features from the penultimate layer with a mean aggregation.

Method implementations.

We predominantly employ PyTorch for implementing all SBL updates and experiments (Paszke et al., 2019), with gradient-based updates making use of the Adam optimizer. We implement random Fourier features as well as Ridge and Huber regression baselines via scikit-learn (Pedregosa et al., 2011), and the exact GP and sparse GP (via inducing points, nind=256n_{\text{ind}}=256) using GPyTorch (Gardner et al., 2018). Robust regression with Student-tt likelihood is manually implemented using EM-style iterative steps with a map-back to Gaussian variance. The BLR is implemented using the IVON optimizer (Shen et al., 2024) to obtain a variational posterior and is iterated with EM steps.

Appendix E Additional Experiment Results

We report additional results, including (i) a comparison of SBL methods on the kernel regression task (Tab. 6, Tab. 7), (ii) full experimental results for tabular regression on all nine datasets for uncontaminated and 10% contamination cases (Tab. 8, Tab. 9, Tab. 10, Tab. 11, Tab. 12), (iii) an additional experiment relating to influence and leverage (Fig. 6), and (iv) baseline results and visual examples for neural network regression (Fig. 7, Fig. 8).
